data.title: HNI Corporation Q1 Preview: Steelcase Integration Needs To Show It Was Worth It
data.description:
Introduction

With the recent decline in HNI Corporation’s (HNI) share price, I wanted to take a look at how it performed throughout 2025 and what is in store for 2026, especially regarding the big acquisition of Steelcase, which I covered in the past. The management has a lot to prove to investors, especially regarding synergies around the acquisition, so that they can justify it. For now, I will be sitting on the fence and waiting to see if the management can prove it was indeed a good investment.

Performance

Looking at the top-line performance, we can see there was a decent jump in the company’s sales, which looks pretty good; however, that is because the last quarter already included some of the acquired company’s sales. Steelcase was acquired by the company at the beginning of December, so the growth of 38% is mostly due to the acquisition. If we strip that out, growth was around 9% y/y. I am expecting a lot higher top-line growth next quarter because in the last quarter, Steelcase’s revenues were only reflecting 24 days under HNI’s operations.

In terms of profitability, we can see that due to the recent acquisition, HNI’s bottom line took a decent beating, but that will not be permanent. We can see that HNI had to pay some acquisition costs of close to $95 million, bringing its net margin down to around 2% from over 5%. So, it is not fair to compare it for now. I will be interested to see how the next quarter looks when we have the full quarter performance of Steelcase.

It is worth noting that the company is in a very tight-margin environment, so even a little improvement on the operating and net margins will go a long way. So, now that we’ve established the profitability, I don’t think we need to delve deeper into other key efficiency metrics solely because it wouldn’t be fair to compare them, given the massive decrease in the bottom line due to distortions of the acquisition. It will be a lot better next quarter, and I’ll be sure to revisit. I can tell you right now that ROA, ROE, and ROTC are all drastically down.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial situation. HNI finished the year with around $209m in cash against $1.28B in long-term debt. The debt pile increased significantly due to the acquisition, but is it worrisome, given the recent operational performance? Even with HNI’s operating down due to the acquisition costs, its interest coverage ratio was still over 3.5x. Many analysts believe that an interest coverage ratio of 2x is healthy, so according to them, HNI is just fine; however, I’d like to see it closer to 5x, as that would allow some room for the years of bad performance and other unforeseen issues. So, at 3.5x it is a bit low, but I think it will improve once Steelcase revenues and income are included next quarter.

The company’s operating cash flow saw some decent growth y/y as well, which is a good sign. Furthermore, even with higher CapEx this year, the company’s free cash flow improved as well.

The picture is not very clear because of the messy inclusion of Steelcase. The full potential is not seen just yet, so it is hard to judge how well the company performed. If we strip out the acquisition costs and exclude everything relating to Steelcase, the company performed about as it did before. It didn’t see much growth in sales, nor an improvement in operations. I am very curious to see what is going to happen next quarter.

Upcoming Quarter

So, analysts are expecting the company to make around 30 cents in EPS on $1.39B in revenues. That is a jump of over 132%, which is much more like it, as Steelcase was similar in operations in terms of revenues. The EPS figure may seem low, especially compared to the previous quarter; however, the management’s guidance of EPS growth was around 14% y/y, so there is still some growth there. It’s just that the first quarter has always been the weaker one overall.

Speaking of management’s guidance, they said that the company will see “modest organic growth,” with the Workplace furnishings segment growing at a mid-single-digit growth rate, while the Residential Building Products segment is expected to grow at a low-single-digit growth rate. The company expects the overall top-line growth rate to be around 130%, which is in line with what the analysts are expecting as well.

I am not expecting a lot of surprises from a furnishing company, even when it consolidated into a much bigger company recently. Organic growth is still going to end up being rather low, but an increase in EPS by double-digit growth does tell me that the company is doing something right operationally, and that is a lot more important than crazy top-line growth, at least for me.

What I’m looking for in the report

As I alluded to earlier, the next report won’t be about the raw revenue jump but more about how the Steelcase integration is going to bring a decent margin expansion instead of just additional scale. The management promised that the synergies would end up saving over $120 million, which is equal to $1.20 in non-GAAP EPS. So, I will look out for how quickly the company will turn that promise into actual operating profit. They said that 2026 accretion should start modestly and then build up over time.

I’m going to be looking out for any comments on whether the integration is going as planned, or is it ahead of plan, or even behind, which happens a lot for many companies. There will certainly be some sort of duplication in corporate costs, so any comments around efficiency will help us see how well the cost savings are going along. Additionally, I will be looking out for any comments surrounding the change in the synergy target, because HNI did say that the estimate is based on current projections that don’t include any revenue synergies.

In the broader sense, I will also be looking for any comments regarding the business segments and how they are going to perform in 2026. The Workplace Furnishings contract demand, pricing, and whether the organic growth will continue to remain positive, excluding the Steelcase acquisition, will be very important to gauge the health of the segment. The whole return-to-office dynamic may still matter, but it is not that important any longer. We know that people are returning to the office. They don’t mind going back in as much as they did before. I think the better question to ask is if the office demand will be stabilizing enough to support steady replacement, refresh, and relocation activity.

In the past, the company mentioned that the business is now being driven by broader workplace investment and portfolio scale than by RTO surge, as it was in the past.

Closing Comments

I think it is too early to judge what the company is going to look like going forward, which is the reason I am going to give it a hold rating. The company needs to show that the acquisition of Steelcase will benefit its operation and not just double its sales numbers. That is not what is important to me. Synergies and savings are the most important part of the business. So, if the management can give me those, I would be happy to invest if the price is right, but right now, I think I’ll let the company do its thing for the next few quarters and revisit once we get to see how the integration progresses.

data.title: Grocery Outlet Holding: Downgrade To Sell On Weakened Fundamentals
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Grocery Outlet Holding (GO) previously with a hold rating, as I wasn't sure about the earnings strength given signs of poor demand and weak legacy store performance. While I did not call for a sell rating, I believed staying on the sideline should be considered a "win" since the stock is down massively from ~$20 to $7 as I write this update. I am now downgrading GO to sell because the story looks worse, not better. GO is no longer dealing with just tech debt. It is now facing a murkier value proposition and a store growth strategy that appears to have gone off track.

The value proposition has slipped

In my previous thesis, the main debate was whether GO could get past its systems disruption fast enough for the order guide, better buying tools, and improved operator stability to improve store productivity. While I was not bullish previously, I could still see a path where it could be done if GO executed well. Fast forward to today; that is no longer just the problem GO is facing.

I believe GO's value proposition is getting murkier, and that directly threatens the reason for GO to trade at a premium multiple (traded at >30x forward PE at one point back in 2022). We can infer this from management's commentary in the Q4 2025 earnings call. Firstly, they noted consumer pressure got worse in Q4 and that weakness carried into Q1. Secondly, they also said customer research showed that while their base pricing remained competitive, value perception had weakened. Lastly, GO's effort to improve in-stocks and everyday assortment has squeezed the supply chain so much that it has reduced the flow of opportunistic product.

That last point is important to note because it means GO has effectively weakened the part of the model that actually drives the shopping experience. Specifically, if you recall, the core value proposition of GO is not to win by looking like a normal discount grocer. GO's differentiation was that it enables a treasure-hunt experience for customers (i.e., customers generally buy a mix of branded and opportunistic deals). When the latter part is being cut off, customers are just buying branded goods, and we know this is true because customers still came in, but they bought fewer items (as implied from the 1.7% drop in average transaction size).

This is one of the biggest reasons for my downgrade to sell. If the issue was still mostly about the tech cleanup, then time alone could solve a lot of it. But if value perception has been damaged, then it is not a matter of time, but a massive effort (resources and cost) to rebuild it. And I would imagine that to be more opportunistic buying, better inventory flow, a return to the previous style of assortment decisions, and a lot of marketing to rebuild that brand image/awareness for shoppers to believe the format is worth the trip again.

All of this takes time, which means we are unlikely to see this happening in the next few quarters. Moreover, they come at a cost, which is going to be painful in the near term. Management's plan for rebuilding opportunistic product levels is to invest more in promotions across branded and fresh categories (noted to spend $20 million of incremental promotional investment in 2026, or ~40 bps of gross margin, with most of it weighted to 1H 2026). To be fair, GO did see early improvement because of this. Management noted comp performance improved by ~100 bps m/m relative to January after these investments started in early February. But I would caution investors not to extrapolate this because it is still a very early data point.

The store growth strategy is not working

The next issue is the store growth strategy. In Q4, GO said it is closing 36 underperforming stores that do not have a viable path to sustained profitability. I don't think this should be framed just as a legacy-store problem. To me, it actually looks more like GO's store growth strategy is simply not working. The bulls can always point to white space and a long runway, but runway alone does not create value.

What matters is whether new stores are opened in the right markets, under the right conditions, and can generate sustainable profits. And from management comments, we can infer that has not been working. The CEO literally said the earlier push for high-single-digit unit growth created challenges and dysfunction, and the CFO also said GO reviewed stores with negative four-wall adj. EBITDA, then looked at real estate quality, competition, execution, and recent trends before deciding which stores to close. Furthermore, management is also changing how it opens stores in newer markets, including using company-run stores first in Virginia before handing them to operators. That tells me GO does not fully trust the old store opening playbook anymore (and the reason why they wouldn't is likely because it doesn't work).

Valuation

While the forward PE multiple has fallen to 13.6x today (from 22x previously), I believe there is still more room for downside given how the fundamentals are shaping up. Core underlying demand is worsening, store opening plans are not working out as expected, and GO is no longer just facing tech debt but also a value proposition problem. Also, management's FY2026 guidance effectively tells us that a near-term recovery is not likely. Specifically, revenue is guided to $4.6 billion to $4.72 billion; comparable sales are guided to between -2% and flat, and importantly, Q1 2026 comparable sales are guided to between -2.5% and -1.5%.

In this sense, I believe GO should trade in line as a normal retailer that has low-single-digit growth like Albertsons Cos. Inc. (ACI), which trades at just 7.5x forward PE. In fact, one would argue that ACI has a better earnings outlook than GO over the next few quarters and should trade at a premium (while GO trades at a discount). Anyway, from 13.6x to 7.5x, that is close to a 45% downside.

Risk

My bearish view could be wrong if GO restores opportunistic product faster than expected and that leads to a quick recovery in basket size, value perception, and comps. The store refresh program could work out better than I expect, and if true, we could start to see a clearer path to recovery in 2026.

Conclusion

I give a sell rating for GO. My view is that the business has deteriorated in two important ways. First, the value proposition looks weaker, which threatens the core reason customers shop at GO and why the stock once traded at a premium multiple. Second, the store growth strategy no longer looks as attractive. With demand under pressure, opportunistic products still needing repair, and FY2026 guidance pointing to no near-term recovery, I think the stock can still see meaningful downside from here.

data.title: Alpine Income Property Trust: Appealing As Both A Dividend Stock And Growth Story
data.description:
A few weeks ago, Raymond James upgraded a REIT with a unique-sounding name, Alpine Income Property Trust, Inc. (PINE). However, this company does not invest in pine trees or the Swiss Alps!

Actually, Pine is a Florida-based REIT, according to its SA profile, with a market cap of $295MM and a focus on “single tenant net leased commercial income properties.”

Since my June 2025 coverage, when I called it a buy, it is up nearly +21%, proving my bullish case and the market's confidence in this REIT. What drove my positive view of this REIT was a diverse tenant exposure nationally, high occupancy, and top-tier tenant names in retail.

This time around, I considered some points from the early-February earnings results, as well as previewing what could be in store for the upcoming late-April earnings call, reviewing this stock across 8 fundamental and technical analysis topics.

Thesis Summary

For my follow-up article today, despite updated research and data, I continue to call this stock a buy, agreeing with the overwhelming bullish consensus heading into the Easter weekend.

My rating worksheet below shows how each of my 8 rating categories drove the overall score and what topics I pulled actionable insights from.

The key “actionable insight” for my readers is that the retail sector could continue to benefit from a strong US consumer, while this specific REIT continues to be on a portfolio growth spree and has shown it can grow FFO as well as dividends, although some technical charting patterns show its recent bullish streak being tested somewhat lately, which is understandable given the market dynamics of the last few weeks.

Macro & Sector Trends

Starting out with a wide view, here I discuss the macro and sector factors impacting this stock, and based on my research, I am bullish here.

From this REIT's property portfolio, we can get a sense that this REIT is quite diversified across a large part of the US, so what comes to mind from a macroeconomic view is what the latest forecasts are saying about consumers and the US economy in the near term, since their retail tenants depend on consumer spending.

One indicator of consumer confidence is the Consumer Confidence Index, which “edged up by 0.8 points in March to 91.8, from 91.0 in February,” according to a March 31st press release from The Conference Board, further highlighting consumer confidence in their incomes, as “19.2% of consumers expected their incomes to increase, up from 18.4% in February.”

Complementing that survey is data published by Business Insider on April 1st, showing a steady positive trend of real personal income growth heading into 2026:

What I think both of the above factors could do is add confidence to the retail sector in the US, despite recent geopolitical headwinds and oil price shocks, since those could turn out to be a temporary impact. If thinking longer-term, a resilient US consumer and the ability to spend are what could favor this REIT.

We can also see from the last investor deck (pg. 5) that this REIT also has portfolio exposure to what I would call “defensive” and necessity-driven retail, like grocery and pharmacy, and that could be an upside factor:

So, given the above factors, I gave the stock a buy rating in this section.

Competitive Positioning

Next, I reviewed how this stock would compare to a peer group sampling, and based on several factors considered, I have a neutral view in this category.

In seeking 3 comparable REITs, I considered a sampling of those exposed to net-lease and single-tenant REITs. I excluded CTO Realty Growth, Inc. (CTO) since they own around 15% equity in PINE and share a common executive, John Albright.

So, I picked NETSTREIT Corp. (NTST), NNN REIT, Inc. (NNN), and Broadstone Net Lease, Inc. (BNL).

I considered some historical performance data of this group, and Netstreit led the top line with a strong 5-year revenue CAGR, but Pine was close behind it at +25.7%. However, it was rosy for return on common equity, with Pine being the only negative one in the group at -1.20% RoCE. These figures could point to Pine having a harder time driving earnings than its peers.

In terms of this stock's 10-year price return vs. the S&P 500, here too it showed a weaker performance than some peers, with a -3.2% return for this period; however, this entire peer group did not seem to have all that much momentum in this period, considerably trailing the benchmark:

Those figures above point to a mixed picture for this stock, but to add another angle to this story, I would also consider some qualitative factors about Pine, and from the investor deck (pg. 5), what grabs my attention is their considerable geographic diversity across many states and US regions (southeast, northeast, midwest, etc.), which I think can support its competitive edge in this peer group.

So, when considering the above factors holistically, I would give this stock a hold rating in this section.

Company Growth Factors

We should consider what could move the top line for this REIT in the near term, and from what I discovered, I am highly bullish.

When thinking of growth drivers in an equity REIT, what I care about most is portfolio growth or optimization, followed by strong tenant retention, and then business acquisition.

Going by what the company reported in the FY25 Results (pg 1-2), it seems they had a net portfolio expansion, with 28 investments in 2025 vs. 21 dispositions. Note that this REIT also seems to originate some commercial lending, but its primary focus is property, and of the new investments, it is seeing a cap rate just above 7%.

From JP Morgan data, cap rates for retail REITs as of Q4 FY25 were in the range of 5-8%, so Pine falls within this spectrum.

The upside driver of portfolio expansion can be future rental income flows, and because leases are long-term, they become a much easier future cash flow predictor than selling a bag of apples.

I would also point out from the investor deck (pg. 4) that this REIT boasts a 99.5% occupancy rate, which I think is materially relevant to this story as it shows a stable tenant demand.

From the Q4 earnings release (pg. 5), the company's outlook for FY26 includes “70MM-100MM” in investment volume, indicating that Pine is on a continuing growth spree.

Complementing that growth story is Seeking Alpha growth data showing expected forward revenue growth of +11.8%, higher than the sector average, but also the fact that a major financial firm like Raymond James recently upgraded them, which I mentioned earlier, as that adds an interesting third-party perspective.

Given these combined factors above, I am a strong buy in this section.

Earnings Outlook

When it comes to factors that could impact earnings, I was modestly bullish.

As I've been writing about equity REITs for a while now, the metrics that I typically point to for their relevance to earnings/profits include FFO/AFFO growth trends and EBITDA margins.

I prefer EBITDA over net profit margins since the latter could see a lot of noise from property depreciation. With that said, Alpine fell behind this peer group with an EBITDA margin of just 75.7%, although the trend line does show an improvement over the last year:

Interestingly, earlier I showed how Pine saw a weaker return on equity than peers, and along with a weaker EBITDA margin, there could be signs of difficulty with driving stronger earnings efficiency from the core business.

Where Pine showed better numbers was a 5-year FFO Growth CAGR of +8.8%, beating the sector average, and they really dominated with a 5-year AFFO CAGR of +12.6%. In fact, it seems the firm is betting that “growth” in 2026 is what will keep driving them forward, with President/CEO John Albright saying in the last earnings results (pg. 1), “We expect 2026 to also generate significant growth, which is reflected in our 2026 earnings guidance.”

It would also be worth noting that the analyst consensus estimate expects +11.6% YoY FFO growth in FY26 and further positive growth in FY27, which I think adds some bright spots to this earnings growth story.

Based on an improving trend in the EBITDA margin, proven FFO/AFFO historic growth achieved, and positive forward outlooks, I gave this section a buy. In the upcoming April earnings call, what will be key to look for is that Pine is executing on its strong guidance so far, because an FFO miss for Q1 could prove trouble for the stock.

Balance Sheet Risk

Moving on to the topic of balance sheet risk, I was bullish in this category.

In this section, I took a closer look at available credit ratings, D/E, and asset risk exposure, which is my usual approach to equity REITs.

While I did not find any recent coverage by major rating agencies, I did pull the D/E metrics, and Pine is the highest of this peer group at 1.38; however, a bright spot is the downward trend forming in its D/E over the last year (dark blue line), which I think could start putting it in a more favorable leverage position if that trend keeps falling.

The risk of too much leverage in this niche cannot be overemphasized, since this business model depends on its large property portfolio generating enough cash flow each year to justify all that debt.

Earlier, I showed that FFO has shown proven growth, and that is a key REIT cash flow driver, but I would also mention that operating cash flow growth saw +9.9% YoY growth, another good sign that this portfolio is driving cash flow, and that helps put the D/E figure into a larger context.

What would be more concerning to me, for instance, would be a very high D/E and poor cash flow metrics shown.

As far as asset risk exposure, in a retail REIT, what I look for is overexposure to one or a few tenants. The data that matters to this topic is the chart of top tenants by ABR (annualized base rent) from the last investor deck (pg. 4), showing the top 5 tenants together make up less than 50% of total ABR, and their top tenant by ABR, Lowe's, is not only a major retail brand in the US but also an investment-grade company, which could lower the risk needle significantly.

So, I think the data tells a story that supports a bullish view: improving D/E, leverage supported by portfolio cash flow, and a diversified tenant base that includes major retail brands as well as “defensive” grocery/pharmacy tenants like Walmart and Walgreens.

Dividend Outlook

The dividend story for this REIT is an interesting one, and here I gave it a bullish view.

Let's consider yield, growth, and dividend coverage.

The yield of +6.5% is respectable and above the sector average, so it could find its way into a high-yield portfolio without hurting that portfolio's average too much.

However, since yield can fluctuate all the time, a more sustainable metric is long-term dividend growth. In that regard, this REIT had a 5-year dividend CAGR of +6%, beating its sector average here too, and I think this shows resilience through various market cycles and shocks.

As far as dividend coverage, the quarterly dividend is $0.30, and the income statement data shows the AFFO/share (diluted) ranging from $0.44 to $0.46 in several quarters in FY25, so that implies an AFFO coverage of 1.46x to 1.53x, which shows adequate coverage.

Based on the combination of factors above, I am a buy in this section too.

Valuation Metrics

Using Seeking Alpha data as inputs, I created the following worksheet, which compares two valuation metrics vs. peers and also uses some of that to come up with a future price forecast for the year ending Dec. 2027.

What sticks out is that Pine is considerably undervalued both on forward P/FFO and trailing P/B multiples. Both matter in this case, since an equity REIT holds a significant asset portfolio. On the one hand, I view it as an undervaluation opportunity since future FFO outlooks are pointing to growth, and the portfolio is exposed to some really strong/major US retail names and is a cash flow grower; however, my upside forecast calls for +2% price growth by Dec. 2027, which is quite muted.

The average Wall Street rating called for a somewhat higher upside at +13.7%, and it shows the street is certainly fond of this stock, not to mention the recent Raymond James upgrade I talked about earlier.

So, I am compelled to be at least neutral/hold in this section, for the reasons above.

Technical Charting

In this final section, I take a break from fundamentals and discuss some technicals, with this chart showing the 1-year price trend vs. the 50-day and 200-day SMA:

This is by far the quickest topic, but certainly not without importance, and it adds an additional angle to the already robust fundamental topics discussed. We can see a buy signal happened this past winter as the 50-day SMA crossed above the 200-day SMA (golden cross formation), and for the most part, the share price followed a bullish pattern, with the 50-day SMA acting as support.

However, that support seems to have been broken slightly recently, but both moving averages continue their upward slope. This pattern best supports a hold/neutral view at this point, with no clear sell signal and the bullish case somewhat pulling back.

While this lowered the stock's overall score, there is still enough reason to be bullish overall on it, and that's what the final outcome ended up being: a buy rating.

Risks and Challenges to Thesis

While my case is a bullish one, a challenge to such a thesis can continue to come from a factor I purposely did not discuss much in this article, to give it a separate discussion here, and that is interest rate risk.

In a REIT with an elevated D/E like this one has, which I showed earlier, additional debt burden at high interest rates is not a great environment to be in, since real estate investment loves low-rate environments on commercial loans.

We can see from income statement data how interest expense has grown for this REIT since March 2025, and this can impact net income.

In fact, the issue is not whether interest rates are high, as they have been elevated for some time, so it is not news, but whether they stay “higher for longer,” and this could, in my view, be one of the biggest risks to an equity REIT.

Conclusion

The way I would best wrap up this discussion of Alpine Income Property Trust is that I believe enough evidence was presented today to show that it could be both a great dividend idea and a growth story as well, so it could appeal to both the income-focused and growth-focused investor, and hence deserves a buy again, particularly while it is priced at a discount to its book value.

data.title: Intel Set To Benefit From Helium Crisis In U.S.-Iran Conflict
data.description:
With the current Middle East conflict entering its second month, it seems more likely this conflict is going to go on for a while, at least several months if not much longer. While the world is focused on oil prices and the umpteenth time some sort of negotiations are mentioned and then dispelled and then refloated, it may be an opportune time to take a look at some less obvious beneficiaries of the current crisis. Helium is mentioned as an item potentially in shortage due to the Hormuz Strait blockade, and I believe that though Intel (INTC) stock price has been flat in the past few months, it is set to become a big beneficiary.

Helium: The Big Shortage

While helium may seem innocuous when used to inflate balloons at parties, it is actually a critical industrial material for high-end medical and technological applications, including the fabrication of semiconductor chips.

The current Middle East conflict knocked out a facility in the Middle East capable of producing helium, removing a third of the global supply and sending semiconductor fabs (among other companies) in a rush for helium. Helium spot prices have reportedly doubled already. But if we look a bit further, INTC is actually set to benefit from this.

According to the United States Geological Survey (USGS) below, the US is the world’s largest producer of helium ((68+13)/180=43% of world production in 2024).

According to the same source, the US extracts its helium from natural gas production and produces more than it consumes, i.e., the US is fully self-sufficient in helium.

Why INTC is Set to Benefit From Helium Crisis

70-75% of the world’s semiconductor chips are produced in East Asia, and these are all reliant on imports of helium; for example, South Korea imports 65% of its helium from Qatar, and Taiwan relies on Qatar for the majority of its helium as well.

But INTC’s helium supplies are sourced from the US. That means it will likely not be impacted; in fact, it may even be able to easily expand production while production in other countries may even decline.

Ever since the COVID pandemic with its vast disruption of the global supply chain over years, surprises are becoming the norm. One year there are not enough chips, the next year there is a shipping bottleneck and chips aren’t being sent over, and the next year there are too many chips, and now it’s not even sure whether the chips are going to be made. At some point it makes sense for companies to keep more of their production near the raw materials, intermediate inputs, and the market, rather than hope a boatful of critical components is shipped halfway across the world three months later and, uh, find out those critical components haven’t been produced because some other boatful of critical materials never made it to the factory to get processed. For example, a tech company might find it just easier to buy the chips from INTC and worry less about disruptions to the global supply chain.

The helium crisis could be a catalyst for INTC to gain market share from its competitors, presenting itself as a reliable producer. Big Tech decision-makers have enough to worry about without having to worry about whether they will get the chips they ordered because crises X, Y, and Z impact production. Predictability is enhanced if they procure more locally from INTC.

INTC Doing Well Even Before Helium Crisis

Helium may act as a catalyst to drive INTC higher. But even without this additional catalyst, INTC has been doing quite well recently:

  • Intel to buy back 49% stake in Ireland fab joint venture for $14.2B, which shows INTC has the confidence to go on the offensive. Previously it sought the backing and funding from external partners to build out its fabs; now it has the resources and the improved operational capacity to regain sole ownership of these fabs.
  • Price hikes by AMD and INTC mentioned: after shortages in GPUs and then memory chips, now there is talk about shortages and price hikes in CPUs, which I previously discussed is a cash cow and a fundamental pillar to INTC’s value.
  • Q4-2025 financial results (revenue and EPS) beat consensus for the 5th consecutive quarter, showing good momentum. While INTC’s stock initially reacted poorly to the news, it is trading near new highs now, showing very strong resilience despite weak overall US stock market performance in recent weeks.

If we go further back, I’ve described my bullish thesis in four articles, ranging from topics on INTC's top-notch management, its turnaround achievements under CEO Lip-Bu Tan, and the favorable macro background for INTC.

  • Intel: The Bull Move Is Just Beginning With 18A Chips Shipment discusses my latest valuation of the company at $280-$740 billion.
  • Buy Intel: U.S.-China Trade Tensions Highlight 'Made In America' Opportunity
  • Will Intel Go Up If It Becomes A 'State-Owned Company'?
  • Intel: Ignore The Tariffs, Buy The Turnaround
  • Why I'm Buying The Intel Stock's Acquisition Rumors

Risks to Bullish Thesis

Now this helium crisis may not persist, and maybe cooler heads prevail and the Middle East reverts back to peace in a few weeks. So this catalyst may not end up propelling INTC for an extended period of time. Chips are still in short supply, and East Asia is still a major (and perhaps preferred) supplier of chips—witness how Korea’s stock market (heavily weighted to chip companies) outperformed in the past few months due to the memory chip boom.

Conclusion

I’ve been a long-time bull on INTC ever since it was c.$20/share in January 2025, and INTC has more than doubled since then. I see current developments in the helium market as very beneficial for INTC, as it combines raw materials, production, and end-user consumption all in one geography and will be the go-to producer for US tech companies if the helium crisis continues for an extended period of time. Even without the disruption in the helium market, I was already very bullish on INTC, and the helium crisis merely acts as a catalyst to remind people of INTC’s value as a semiconductor fab located in its home country in an era of turbulence

data.title: Citigroup: Attractively Valued Ahead Of The 2026 Investor Day
data.description:
Introduction

Despite posting a small loss so far in 2026, shares in Citigroup (C) have outperformed the broader iShares US Financials ETF (IYF), benefiting from the bank's undemanding valuation relative to large peers such as JPMorgan Chase (JPM). I see Citigroup as attractively valued ahead of Q1 2026 earnings and the upcoming 2026 Investor Day on May 7, ranking it with a Buy rating. The bullish thesis can be summarized as:

  • Citigroup trades at an attractive 11.3x 2026 earnings multiple, a circa 17% discount to large peers such as JPM.
  • At the same time, Citigroup's earnings growth post 2026 is likely to beat peers, largely driven by a low starting base in terms of return on equity.
  • Citigroup's provisioning is in line with the Fed's 2026 outlook and recent U.S. economic data, potentially pointing to no sizable provision building over the near term, notwithstanding risks from international market exposure.

Q1 2026 Earnings Preview

Citigroup is due to report earnings on April 14, with analysts expecting EPS of $2.61/share. This would represent a notable ~33% Y/Y increase relative to the $1.96/share achieved in the prior year quarter.

I calculate that roughly 7% of the EPS growth should come from a lower share count, benefiting from share repurchases executed in 2025. A smaller loss in the All Other (managed basis) segment should also help, with Citigroup making progress on overseas divestitures in 2025.

Provisioning related to the United States exposure should remain contained, as Citigroup's allowance for credit losses reflected a Q1 2026 unemployment rate of 4.5%, versus a 4.3% unemployment rate in the March 2026 employment report. Hence, the absence of the $210 million provision build observed in Q1 2025 could boost EPS by about $0.12/share, accounting for roughly 6% of the EPS increase.

As with most banks, Citigroup's annual report does not provide a detailed exposure overview of the Middle East (as the region is bundled in one group alongside Russia and Africa). However, some provision build related to the ongoing war in the region is quite likely, potentially negating the benefits from a strong performance in the United States.

To sum up, roughly 40% of Citigroup's Q1 2026 EPS growth is likely to come from low-hanging fruit such as a lower share count and a normalization in provisioning, with the remaining 60% dependent on business growth and benefits from overseas divestitures last year.

2026 Investor Day

After Q1 2026 earnings, attention will quickly turn to the May 7 Investor Day. Citigroup is widely expected to announce new medium term profitability targets. Indeed, with a return on tangible common equity [ROTCE] of 4.9% to 8.9% in 2022-2025, the bank's profitability remains notably below peers. For comparison, Wells Fargo (WFC) targets an ROTCE of 17-18% over the medium term, while JPMorgan Chase has achieved an ROTCE of 20-22% in recent years.

While no one expects Citigroup to reach JPM's profitability in a few years' time, the principal driving force behind a potential improvement in Citigroup's profitability will be a substantial reduction in the $5.5 billion loss in the "All Other" segment, reduced to $4.5 billion on a managed basis once we account for one-off effects such as the impairment of goodwill in Mexico.

For 2026, Citigroup expects ROTCE to improve to 10-11%, with my calculations showing that a gradual winddown of losses in the All Other segment would allow Citigroup to reach an ROTCE of about 13%. Coupled with ongoing operational and capital efficiency improvements, I see a ROTCE of 13-14% as a realistic target for Citigroup's profitability over the next few years.

Valuation and Rating

Analysts expect Citigroup to deliver EPS of $10.23/share in 2026, reflecting an undemanding 11.3x forward P/E for the shares. This still represents a circa 17% discount relative to JPM's forward P/E of about 13.6x, with JPM's future earnings growth arguably subdued in light of the already robust profitability currently in place.

In contrast, as long as Citigroup continues to make incremental improvements in its efficiency and reduces losses in the All Other segment, I see Citigroup as posting above-average earnings growth over the medium term. This is also reflected in analyst estimates, with Citigroup EPS seen growing almost 19% in 2027 versus 8% for JPM.

In essence, notwithstanding risks that I will discuss shortly, Citigroup presents a compelling mix between attractive current valuations and above-average future earnings growth, earning a Buy rating on my part.

Risks

Despite a renewed focus on the U.S. home market, revenues from international markets still account for roughly 49% of Citigroup's topline. As such, the bank is arguably more exposed to weakness in overseas economies (at least compared to peers such as Wells Fargo, which gets 95% of its revenue from the U.S.), which is currently driven by the war in the Middle East. As such, higher provisioning for international markets may offset a stabilization in provisioning at home.

Turning to the United States, while Citigroup's macroeconomic assumptions are largely in line with recent data and the Fed's own outlook, underperformance relative to expectations may lead to a new provision-building cycle, putting pressure on Citigroup's bottom line even if the bank manages to achieve efficiency improvements in terms of costs and capital.

Last but not least, the third principal risk to consider is that Citigroup will face substantial execution risks in achieving the (potentially higher) profitability target set at the Investor Day next month. As such, there is no guarantee that the bank will manage to build on profitability improvements after 2026.

Conclusion

Citigroup shares have posted a small loss so far in 2026, outperforming most U.S. financial peers. I think this performance is largely warranted, as Citigroup trades at an undemanding valuation relative to its expected 2026 earnings, with markets giving the bank little credit for potential profitability improvements in subsequent years.

Analysts expect a substantial Y/Y increase in Q1 2026 EPS, with roughly 40% of gains driven by low-hanging fruit such as a lower share count and provision normalization, with the remainder more dependent on business performance and optimizing costs in the All Other segment.

I expect Citigroup to announce an ROTCE target of about 13-14% at its Investor Day, principally driven by overseas divestments coupled with capital and operational efficiency improvements. While this would still rank Citigroup below major peers, its more attractive valuation should still allow the bank to outperform nevertheless.

Higher provisions in international and the home U.S. markets are a key risk in the investment case. Investors should also take note of the inherent uncertainty and execution risks coming with achieving a higher ROTCE over the medium term.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: The 7.3% Dividend Of The Preferred Stock Of Bank OZK Is Highly Attractive
data.description:
Almost three years ago, I strongly recommended buying the preferred stock of Bank OZK (OZKAP) for its 7.8% dividend yield back then and the wide margin of safety of the dividend. I also expected some upside, given my expectations for lower interest rates. Indeed, interest rates have moderated since that article, from 5.5% to 3.75%. As a result, the preferred stock has offered a total return of 31%, which is certainly satisfactory for a low-risk, fixed-income security and a period of less than three years.

Since that article, I have reiterated my positive view on the preferred stock of Bank OZK three times. I last recommended buying the stock in late 2024. The stock had remained essentially flat until recently, but it has declined 5% off its peak in February, primarily due to the war in the Middle East and the resultant expectations for higher inflation and possibly higher interest rates in the upcoming months.

Due to its recent decline, the preferred stock of Bank OZK is currently offering a dividend yield of 7.3%. The preferred dividend has a wide margin of safety (more on this later). The reason behind the high yield is the fear of the investing community that the ongoing war in Iran, which has caused oil prices to skyrocket, will result in higher inflation and interest rates. If this proves correct, the preferred stock of Bank OZK is likely to come under pressure, along with most fixed-income securities.

However, the war is unlikely to last for many more months, as both sides are bleeding severely. Iran is being bombarded harshly, while the U.S. has begun to feel the impact of the war via sky-high gasoline prices due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Given the upcoming midterm elections, the U.S. government will do its best to prevent citizens from becoming frustrated over extreme gasoline prices. In addition, the major oil producers in the Middle East are being severely hurt by the closure of the straits, as they have been forced to drastically reduce their output. Therefore, the current situation, which appears unsustainable for all the parties involved, seems to be presenting a great opportunity to lock in an essentially safe 7.3% yield and wait patiently for the war to come to an end.

Safety of the Preferred Dividend

When a stock offers an above-average dividend yield, it usually signals that the dividend is not safe. However, this is not the case for the preferred stock of Bank OZK. Bank OZK does not have the right to pay a common dividend if it suspends or cuts its preferred dividend. However, Bank OZK has an outstanding record of common dividends. More precisely, the bank has raised its common dividend for 28 consecutive years, including 63 consecutive quarters. The 28-year period includes the Great Recession, which was the worst financial crisis of the past 80 years; the fierce recession caused by the pandemic in 2020; and the downturn of regional banks in 2023, which resulted from the bankruptcy of three regional banks, namely Silicon Valley Bank, Signature Bank, and First Republic Bank.

The resilience of Bank OZK throughout all these downturns is a testament to the defensive business model of the bank and the high quality of its management. Notably, the CEO of Bank OZK, George Gleason, has remained in his position over the last 47 years (!) and has always prioritized maintaining a healthy loan portfolio.

Despite its sustained growth, the common dividend of Bank OZK still has a wide margin of safety. To be sure, the trailing payout ratio is standing at 29%, which is better than the 35% median payout ratio of the financial sector and provides a wide margin of safety.

The recent business performance of Bank OZK indicates that there is no reason to worry about the safety of the common and preferred dividends. In the fourth quarter of 2025, the bank grew its loans and deposits 8% each over the prior year’s quarter. It also grew its net interest income 7% thanks to lower deposit costs amid lower interest rates and maintained a conservative loan-to-deposit ratio of 97%. Provisions for credit losses increased from $37.2 million to $50.6 million, but earnings per share dipped only 2%, from $1.56 to $1.53.

The earnings per share of $1.53 were just 4% lower than the all-time high earnings per share of $1.59 reported in the previous quarter. It is also remarkable that Bank OZK has posted record earnings per share in each of the last five years. While the company may not achieve another all-time high this year, it will almost certainly remain highly profitable, given its high-quality business model and its positive business performance. Analysts expect the bank to post just a 3% decrease in its earnings per share this year and achieve record earnings in 2027 and 2028.

It is remarkable that Bank OZK is a major bank in its home operating area, Arkansas, and thus it enjoys a significant competitive advantage. This is clearly reflected in the net interest margin of the bank, which has remained much wider than that of its peers over the last several years. In each of the last nine quarters, Bank OZK has posted a net interest margin between 4.30% and 4.82%, whereas the average net interest margin of the financial industry has been 3.16%-3.39%.

Source: Investor Presentation

Moreover, as shown in the above slide, Bank OZK has always tried to synchronize deposit growth to fund growth in loans. This is clearly reflected in the growth of loans and deposits in each of the last three years, as these two metrics have grown at essentially the same pace throughout this period. The sustained conservative loan-to-deposit ratio provides additional resilience during downturns.

Notably, the primary business of Bank OZK is funding large-scale commercial real estate projects. These projects can be potentially vulnerable to a fierce recession. However, management has always paid special attention to maintaining a high-quality loan portfolio. These loans currently comprise 54% of the total loans of the bank.

Source: Investor Presentation

As shown above, Bank OZK maintains conservative leverage in its large-scale commercial real estate loans. These loans currently have a loan-to-cost ratio of 49% and a loan-to-value ratio of 46%. As a result, even if a recession shows up, the bank is likely to be able to absorb an appreciable amount of write-offs. The aforementioned resilience of the bank to all kinds of downturns and its all-time high earnings per share in each of the last five years confirm the high quality of the loan portfolio of the bank. Thanks to its high-quality business model, Bank OZK is not likely to cut its common or preferred dividend even in the adverse scenario of sustained high inflation or a recession.

To cut a long story short, the common dividend of Bank OZK has a wide margin of safety. As a side note, the preferred stock of Bank OZK is non-cumulative, i.e., any forgone dividends will not be distributed whenever preferred dividends are reinstated. However, as the preferred dividend cannot be cut or suspended unless the common dividend is suspended as well, it is evident that the preferred dividend of Bank OZK is essentially safe.

Risk of Interest Rates

The only material risk for the preferred shareholders of Bank OZK is the risk of higher interest rates in the future. The ongoing war in Iran has led oil prices to skyrocket due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Under normal conditions, nearly 20% of global oil consumption passes through these straits. Consequently, as long as these straits remain blocked by Iran, oil prices are likely to remain exceptionally high.

If this crisis lasts for several months, it is likely to cause a resurgence of inflation, as the cost of energy affects the cost of several goods and services. If inflation breaks decisively above 3.0%, it will probably lead the Fed to raise interest rates. Such a development will be negative for fixed-income securities, including the preferred stock of Bank OZK. Higher interest rates are likely to cause an increase in credit losses, as borrowers will find it harder to service their debt. In addition, higher interest rates exert pressure on the valuation of fixed-income securities to align their yields with the prevailing yields in the market. Overall, in the event of a prolonged war in Iran, the preferred stock of Bank OZK is likely to remain under pressure.

However, the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is unlikely to last for more than a few months. Due to its severe consequences, the U.S. government has shifted its stance lately and is now doing its best to put an end to this crisis. Given the upcoming midterm elections, the U.S. government will do its best to drive gasoline prices back to normal; otherwise, it runs the risk of facing negative results in these elections. Not only is the U.S. being hurt by the ongoing crisis, but also Iran is bleeding as well, as its infrastructure is being bombarded. In addition, all the major oil producers in the Middle East are suffering, as they cannot export their output due to the closure of Hormuz. All these countries rely heavily on oil revenues to fund their budgets.

Overall, the current situation has severe repercussions for the U.S. and nearly every country in the Persian Gulf. As a result, it is likely that all these countries will do their best to put an end to this crisis relatively soon. A deal between Iran and Iraq has just been announced. According to this deal, Iran will allow the cargoes of Iraqi oil to pass through the straits of Hormuz without any problem. This agreement probably signals that the countries in the Middle East will do their best to debottleneck the area and return to normal operation. Therefore, the negative scenario of a sustained rebound of inflation and interest rates has low odds of materializing.

Call Risk

Some preferred stocks have the risk of being called by the issuing company, but this is not the case for the preferred stock of Bank OZK. This stock becomes callable in November 2026. However, it is currently trading at a 36% discount to its par value of $25. As a result, it will offer excessive capital gains to the preferred shareholders if it is called at some point in the future.

The preferred stock of Bank OZK was issued in 2021, when interest rates were nearly zero. In the absence of a significant decrease in interest rates, the preferred stock of Bank OZK is unlikely to be called. Nevertheless, if interest rates decrease significantly from their current level of 3.75% in the future, the preferred stock of Bank OZK is likely to offer material capital gains to its shareholders, on top of its attractive dividend yield. Even if interest rates remain flat, the preferred stock of Bank OZK is likely to retrieve its recent war-driven losses (~4%) whenever the war comes to an end.

Final Thoughts

Economic crises usually present great opportunities to lock in exceptionally high yields for the long run. This rule seems to apply to the preferred stock of Bank OZK. The stock is offering a 7.3% dividend yield with a wide margin of safety. It may incur some pressure in the short run due to the war in Iran and the resultant risk of a spike of inflation, but this downturn is likely to prove short-lived. The 7.3% preferred yield of Bank OZK appears attractive for income-oriented investors who can maintain a long-term perspective during the ongoing crisis in the Middle East.

data.title: My Thoughts On Momentum Investing
data.description:
I believe that more money can be made by buying high and selling at even higher prices. I take exception to the idea of buying low and selling high - Richard Driehaus, the father of momentum investing

Preface

Allow me to start by stating unequivocally that I am not a dedicated momentum investor. Nor am I a dedicated value investor. In fact, if you want to put a label on me, I am probably best described as a thematic investor. I believe in certain themes (aka megatrends) taking control of the world we live in and, if you can identify and invest correctly in those themes, it actually makes little difference whether the companies you end up investing in can best be described as one or the other.

In last month's Absolute Return Letter – How (Not) to Value Equities – which you can find here , I made the point that momentum investors shouldn't be overly concerned about the point I made, i.e. that the returns on U.S. equities over the next decade are likely to fall dramatically short of the returns we have gotten used to in recent years.

I have had a few comments and questions on the back of that argument. It is therefore only natural that I elaborate on the point I made. What do I really mean when I say that momentum investors shouldn't pay too much attention to how expensive markets are? And which signals do I use to detect when, suddenly, it isn't irrelevant anymore? In this month's Absolute Return Letter, I will dig deeper on those two questions.

A bit of history

I am old enough to remember the boom and bust in Japan in the late 1980s and early 1990s and, likewise, the dotcom boom and bust in the U.S. some ten years later. I think of those two incidents virtually every day, as I remind myself of not falling into the same trap again.

Momentum investors had a feast in Japan in the late 1980s, and they enjoyed it no less in USA a decade later. I was the new kid on the block in Copenhagen when Nationalbanken (the Central Bank of Denmark), in January 1984, relaxed the rules to do with investing abroad. For the first time ever, ordinary Danes were allowed to invest in non-Danish equities.

Coincidentally, the 'party' in Japan started at about the same time. Day after day, and with few questions asked, clients filled their pockets with Japan equities. A new generation of momentum investors had been born. About ten years later, the story repeated itself although, this time, it was all about a new phenomenon we hardly understood. Those who did, called it the internet . Again, momentum investors made fortunes on companies we had never heard of before.

Everything was fine until, suddenly, it wasn't. In Japan, the party ended abruptly in 1990 due to a combination of government policy tightening and structural weaknesses in Japan's financial system which had been exposed. Ten years later, in USA, the story broadly repeated itself. A worried Fed had increased the policy rate no less than six times between the summer of 1999 and the spring of 2000, and, suddenly, financial markets snapped.

However, the point I want to make is a different one. At least two years before the party ended, both in Japan and in USA, you could have made exactly the same argument. Take for example the dotcom boom. Had you invested exclusively in the Nasdaq index to participate in the boom (as a dedicated momentum investor would have done) but sold it all at the end of 1997, as somebody had told you valuations were now ridiculous (as they were), you would have missed +40% in 1998 and +86% in 1999. In other words, exiting prematurely is associated with significant career risk.

Exhibit 1a: S&P 500 momentum relative to S&P 500 since 1972

Exhibit 1b: MSCI World momentum relative to MSCI World since 1972

Take a quick look at the charts above. As you can see, in the US (Exhibit 1a), momentum investing has enjoyed a fabulous 54 years since 1972. Only in the first few years after the dotcom bust in 2000 did momentum investors significantly underperform; however, over the entire period, momentum investors have performed dramatically better than index investors – by a factor 5x.

The picture is modestly different in Exhibit 1b (global equities). In the first three decades, momentum actually underperformed; however, since the early 2000s, momentum investors have outperformed index investors when investing globally and, over the entire period, they have outperformed by a factor 3x.

The father of momentum investing in a few words

Richard Driehaus, who unexpectedly died in 2021, is widely recognised as the father of momentum investing. He identified and bought stocks when they have strong upward price momentum and held on to them, as long as the momentum continued. He focused on small- and mid-cap companies with accelerating earnings growth and emphasised companies that are capable of delivering significant, positive earnings surprises.

Key to his success was his ability to hold on to his winners whilst quickly getting rid of his losers – something we could all learn from. Another key was his willingness to diversify during bad times and to concentrate his holdings during good times. He had four key metrics he followed religiously:

  1. positive earnings surprises;
  2. sharp upwards revisions in consensus earnings estimates;
  3. accelerating earnings & sales; and
  4. very strong, consistent and sustained earnings growth.

Even better if the earnings growth was not only year-on-year but also sequential; however, of the four metrics, Driehaus probably assigned most value to #1. If you want to learn more about the methodology conducted by Richard Driehaus, I suggest you read this 2021 article in Forbes Magazine .

What have I learned so far and what can I learn?

In terms of how to deal with seemingly overvalued equity markets, by far the most important lesson I have learnt from a long career in the industry is that booms don't turn to busts just because equities are overvalued. A catalyst shall be required. As I pointed out earlier, in the two incidents mentioned in this month's letter, aggressive monetary tightening did the trick.

That said, the U.S. monetary policy regime has changed since the late 1990s; the focus is no longer on inflation only. Adding to that, U.S. households own colossal amounts of equities. It would take a man with nerves of steel to end this party. Furthermore, the current tenant of the White House is (i) addicted to debt and (ii) prepared to fire FOMC members who don't dance to his tune. All of this means that the current (seemingly illogical) behaviour can continue for much longer than most of us expect, and that the catalyst, when it eventually arrives, will most likely be something we hadn't thought of.

This doesn't imply you shouldn't take your precautions. In last month's Absolute Return Letter, I listed five particular lines of action we have taken to take risk out of our portfolio. We:

  1. 1. significantly reduced our exposure to U.S. equities, the most expensive on Earth;
  2. 2. didn't go into cash but instead increased our exposure to Europe, Canada, Japan and China;
  3. 3. significantly lowered the equity beta in our portfolio;
  4. 4. increased the exposure to certain commodities (mostly industrial metals); and we
  5. 5. bought gold.

Looking forward, what is the most important danger signal that I look out for? There are obviously many – financial magazines putting a big story on the front page is always a good contrarian indicator – but one stands out to me. Going back to momentum investing and Richard Driehaus, when investors start to react negatively

unless the positive earnings surprise is substantial, all the red lamps start flashing in my office. Unfortunately, I have seen a few of those in recent weeks. Now, one swallow doesn't make a summer, but it is an indicator I follow keenly, and I will strongly suggest you do the same.

Niels
