data.title: Moderna: Speculative Buy For Aggressive Investors Betting On mRNA's Future
data.description:
I decided to do a postmortem on my November 20, 2023 buy recommendation for Moderna (MRNA). That recommendation didn't work out too well; the stock dropped 37.20% from article publication to its March 30 closing price of $48.23.

My thesis for buying Moderna was based on speculation about the success of the company's mRNA platform and drug pipeline. I had also factored in the downside from the decline in the number of people getting COVID-19 vaccinations; Moderna had called for a 2024 annual low of $4 billion.

The actual results were far worse: the company's COVID-19 (Spikevax) sales for the full year 2024 were $3.1 billion, missing company estimates. Its RSV sales were also a major disappointment. The company generated only $25 million in 2024 RSV (mRESVIA) sales. Additionally, Moderna lacked new drugs in its pipeline to offset declining revenue from COVID-19 vaccines.

The market also disliked Moderna's declining profits; it was last profitable in late 2023, highlighting post-COVID revenue declines and high research and development (R&D) spending on its pipeline.

Once the market realized the company would remain unprofitable and burn cash for several years, investors quickly exited their positions. The stock fell to a 52-week low of $22.28 on November 21, 2025. However, it has since rebounded.

The stock price is up over 70% year-to-date (as of March 31, 2026). There are reasons that some are getting excited about the stock again and reasons to invest in it today.

Moderna may have already passed its peak spending phase. Some of its most expensive Phase III trials are nearing completion, particularly in the seasonal vaccine space, and future R&D expenses should decline. Although it will continue to invest in cancer drug trials, developing cancer drugs is less costly than developing viral vaccines. Therefore, its profitability should begin improving moving forward.

Senior VP & Head of Investor Relations, Lavina Talukdar, said at the Barclays 28th Annual Global Healthcare Conference:

The cost of oncology Phase III development or Phase II development is much, much lower than the cost of developing vaccines because of the sheer number of participants in vaccine studies versus the patients that we treat in the studies for oncology. And so all of that being said, we do anticipate that costs overall, cash cost for the company will be coming down to the tune of $3.5 billion to $3.7 billion in 2027, which would mean that we would need to be in that range for revenue in 2028 to breakeven.

In other words, if the company can achieve revenue of $3.5 to $3.7 billion in 2028, it may reach breakeven that year. Since the company has several drugs in the pipeline that could help it reach that revenue level, some analysts have already started penciling in positive earnings per share for 2029.

Some investors may decide to wait until Moderna becomes profitable again before investing. However, that may be the wrong strategy. The market will begin to rerate the stock higher once it believes the path to profitability is viable. In fact, that process may have already started. Among the reasons the stock is well above its November 2025 low and has continued higher in 2026 is that some believe the company has enough viable drugs in its pipeline to reach $3.5 to $3.7 billion in revenue.

Let's look at some of the drugs in Moderna's pipeline.

Moderna's Pipeline

Moderna is moving beyond being known as just a vaccine company; it is becoming a major player in oncology. One big advantage that oncology drugs (especially personalized ones) have over respiratory vaccines like COVID-19, flu, and RSV is that they have reliable demand, face less competition, and command premium pricing. Respiratory vaccines are the complete opposite: they are seasonal, demand can be inconsistent, they face heavy competition, and they are subject to pricing pressure. Investors will likely assign a higher valuation to a successful oncology drug than to a respiratory vaccine.

The oncology drug that is furthest advanced is Intismeran autogene mRNA-4157 (also known by its commercial name of V940), which is currently in phase III trials for adjuvant melanoma (fully enrolled), adjuvant non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), and adjuvant NSCLC non-pCR post-neoadjuvant. Adjuvant means post-surgery.

These indications could be big needle movers for Moderna and significantly help the company reach its $3.5 to $3.7 billion revenue goal by 2028. Senior VP Talukdar said at the Barclay conference:

So in adjuvant melanoma, the way we characterize the market opportunity there is worldwide, it's a multibillion-dollar opportunity. We are 50-50 with our partners, Merck. And in that scenario, we would still consider our share as a multibillion-dollar opportunity at peak worldwide.

Moderna is co-developing Intismeran autogene mRNA-4157, a Personalized Cancer Vaccine (PCV), with Merck (MRK), and the program has shown encouraging results so far for melanoma, a skin cancer. PCVs have high upside potential because they are custom manufactured for each patient's tumor mutations. Consequently, Merck/Moderna can charge patients higher prices for this treatment, and it can't be easily copied by competitors, creating a moat around it.

Assuming mRNA-4157 reports positive interim clinical results from INTerpath‑001 (phase III trial) in 2026, Moderna and Merck could potentially release mRNA-4157 for melanoma in 2027. Still, the biggest upside could come from the possibility that it could be used for other solid tumors like lung cancer, bladder cancer, pancreatic cancer, and gastric cancer.

The company is also expanding into first-line treatments; specifically, it already has Intismeran autogene mRNA-4157 in phase II trials for first-line metastatic melanoma and first-line metastatic squamous NSCLC. Generally, first-line treatments have larger patient populations than adjuvant treatments, which means a greater revenue-generating opportunity. If Intismeran autogene mRNA-4157 succeeds in all of its indications, which may take three to five years to play out, Moderna may be a hot stock again by 2029.

In the company's infectious disease portfolio, the indication investors have been most eyeing is mRNA-1403, its Norovirus vaccine. Norovirus often comes from contaminated food, and some call it "stomach flu." Data Intelo estimates the Norovirus market size at $1.8 billion in 2025 and forecasts it to grow to $4.7 billion by 2034, a compound annual growth rate ("CAGR") of 11.2%. So, at its peak, it represents a multibillion-dollar revenue opportunity if Moderna can achieve first-mover status in the space.

Moderna is highly interested in Norovirus because it is a greenfield opportunity; there are currently no approved vaccines for Norovirus worldwide. There is also strong commercial demand for a preventive solution due to the frequency of breakouts in key settings such as cruise ships, military barracks, and long-term care facilities. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention ("CDC") stated on its website in 2024, "Each year, there are about 2,500 reported norovirus outbreaks in the United States." Additionally, there are 685 million total cases of acute gastroenteritis globally.

Senior VP Talukdar said at the Barclay conference:

So norovirus, we just announced that it was fully enrolled in that second Northern Hemisphere season. And we're accruing cases. So this is another event-driven or case accrual dependent readout. We do anticipate that, that will happen in 2026. In terms of what we expect on the vaccine efficacy side there, because there aren't any products or any vaccines approved for norovirus, we do think that a minimum of a 50% vaccine efficacy rate would be needed to get this product through and on to the market. And that's because you've had other vaccines come to market with a 50% vaccine efficacy rate. Obviously, we're shooting for better than that, but a minimum of 50% is what we think you would need to come to market.

Moderna believes it can succeed in this area where others have failed, due to its focus on Seropositive (presence of antibodies for the virus in blood serum tests) adults. The company believes mRNA is very effective at protecting adults previously exposed to Norovirus. Programs that have failed in the past from other companies have chosen to develop vaccines for children or infants, which is much harder because success in immunizing against Norovirus often requires multiple exposures to provide protection. It is much easier to protect adults previously exposed to Norovirus than infants or children who have never been exposed.

Additionally, several previous attempts to develop a Norovirus vaccine have used recombinant proteins. Moderna believes it has a better solution: its mRNA platform, which is designed to teach the body to produce specific proteins that can trigger a stronger, more targeted immune response.

What to Look for Moving Forward

Most of Moderna's upside is tied to the success of its mRNA-4157 phase III trial results. Investors should monitor company press releases and management commentary for progress in phase III of its melanoma cancer vaccine indication. If it succeeds with mRNA-4157, Moderna should generate more reliable, steady, high-margin revenue within several years.

Since the COVID-19 vaccine has been in decline over the last several years, some may discount the importance of the company's new COVID vaccine, mNEXSPIKE. However, the COVID-19 vaccine remains important to Moderna's revenue growth plans. The company hopes international growth can revive COVID vaccine sales this year, as the U.S. Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., a known anti-vaccine activist, has disrupted sales in the U.S. market.

Some of the reasons the company's international COVID sales have suffered over the last few years include being locked out of Europe. At the beginning of 2027, the company should be able to sell mNEXSPIKE in Europe. Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Stéphane Bancel said at the 44th Annual J.P. Morgan Healthcare Conference:

So if you think about the geographic growth we're going to get from a basis standpoint in U.K., Canada, Australia and the mNEXSPIKE continued growth in the U.S. and new growth outside the U.S., that will help Moderna grow for the first time in a few years in 2026. Then if you look into 2027, which we're already preparing right now, we are quite excited about the opportunities ahead of us. And the first one is in Europe. As some of you know or might remember, we have been excluded from the European market for now several years because of a partnership between Pfizer (PFE) and the EU, which expires at the end of '26. So we're going to get access again to the [COVID-19] market, which is a very big market in Europe. In Europe, you have 90 million people compared to 60 million people in the U.S. above 65 years of age. And the price differential on vaccine is not very, very high. So the volume and the number of people you can treat is actually really important.

The opening up of Europe is the prelude to the company becoming a first mover in the European "winner-take-all" flu-plus-COVID combo market. CEO Bancel believes the company's flu-plus-COVID combo vaccine, mCombriax, for adults 50 and older, will become one of Moderna's most important growth engines in 2027.

CEO Bancel said at the J.P. Morgan conference (which took place on January 12, 2026) :

The other piece that could be very, very helpful to us in terms of sales growth in '27 is the flu plus COVID combo. If we're able to get an approval of flu plus COVID combo in Europe in 2026 that will allow us to get [National Immunization Technical Advisory Groups (NITAGs)] recommendations, so CDC equivalent recommendations and pricing negotiation to be able for the winter of '27, '28 to be able to be in the market. And most probably given where other products are right now, we will be the only product in the market with flu plus COVID combo.

On February 27, 2026, European regulators ("CHMP") gave mCombriax marketing authorization in the European Union ("EU"), which essentially means the combo vaccine is approved in the EU for adults 50 and older. Since its prospective competitors won't have a vaccine ready for the 2027 and 2028 flu seasons, Moderna will essentially have a monopoly in the market.

Another area investors should monitor is the company's margin improvement. Earlier, I mentioned that the company's expected reduction in R&D costs should improve profitability. However, lower R&D isn't the only reason that Moderna's margins should improve. Re-entering the European market doesn't just bump up revenue. By spreading fixed costs over more European jabs, the company can increase its gross margins.

Management also plans to improve margins by taking advantage of the cyclicality of respiratory vaccines; it will manufacture non-respiratory products (such as rare disease and cancer vaccines) during the first and second quarters, the slow periods for respiratory vaccine sales (COVID, RSV, and flu), which would theoretically increase factory utilization by decreasing the time factories are idle. Increasing factory utilization is another way to reduce the fixed cost per additional unit manufactured, thereby increasing gross margins.

CEO Bancel made this comment at the J.P. Morgan event:

Then when we have non-vaccine product to launch like PA [Propionic Acidemia], mRNA-4359 [melanoma and NSCLC], mRNA-2808 [multiple myeloma], those products will be made off-season of respiratory vaccine. Because today, if you think about it, we are really penalized in terms of gross margin because in Q1, the factory is not very busy because we supply the Southern Hemisphere, it's not big volumes. And so you could make PA product or any of those other products that are not individualized in your off-season time line, absorbing your fixed cost. So that will be helping.

Another way the company plans to improve margins is by developing in-house capabilities for functions that are currently outsourced. CEO Bancel said at the J.P. Morgan event:

We are bringing for the U.S. market prefilled syringe manufacturing in Norwood. And so this also should improve margin because today, we pay a third party and we pay the margin and we pay the taxes. When we bring this, we're actually going to amortize the fixed cost of Norwood, including the assets, the building which we own and the personnel.

Moderna also plans to negotiate better deals with suppliers and use artificial intelligence to boost productivity. These margin-improving tactic changes will take time, but investors should expect steady margin improvements over the next three to four years. It’s important to keep track of the company’s results to see if margins are improving as expected.

Balance Sheet

Moderna ended the fourth quarter of 2025 with $5.80 billion in Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP) cash and short-term investments. However, if you listen to the company's fourth quarter 2025 earnings call, Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") James Mock said:

We ended 2025 with cash and investments of $8.1 billion, compared to $9.5 billion at the end of 2024. The year-over-year decrease was primarily driven by operating losses as we continue to invest in R&D and advance our pipeline, partially offset by the $600 million initial draw of our $1.5 billion credit facility. Excluding the credit facility draw, we would have ended the year with $7.6 billion of cash and investments, which was above our 3Q guidance of $6.5 billion to $7 billion due to lower operating expenses, lower capital expenditures and working capital improvements.

The CFO's comments include a liquidity measure that adds non-current investments of $2.336 billion to GAAP cash and short-term investments of $5.80 billion, for a total of $8.14 billion.

YCharts shows long-term debt of $1.288 billion. However, the company's fourth quarter 2025 earnings release shows it has $590 million in long-term debt, $653 million in operating leases, and $20 million in financing leases, for a total of $1.263 billion, which is close to the $1.288 billion on the following YCharts. YCharts historically adds leases to long-term debt in its long-term debt calculation.

The company detailed its long-term debt in its 2025 10-K:

In November 2025, we entered into the Credit Agreement providing for a senior secured term loan facility with aggregate commitments of $1.5 billion, consisting of a $600 million initial term loan and $900 million of delayed draw term loan commitments. As of December 31, 2025, the $600 million initial term loan was outstanding and matures on November 24, 2030. Borrowings under the Credit Agreement bear interest at a variable rate. Interest amounts preset in the table are based on the applicable interest rate in effect as of December 31, 2025. The principal amount of $600 million is due in full at maturity, and no principal payments are required prior to that date.

Moderna's debt-to-equity (D/E) ratio is 0.151, meaning the company conservatively finances its operations with equity more than debt. However, if you divide the company's long-term debt of $590 million (excluding operating leases) by its equity of $8.65 billion, its D/E is 0.06.

Moderna has a current ratio of 3.293, indicating the company has sufficient liquidity to meet its short-term obligations. However, be aware that under the CFO's liquidity framing, which includes non-current investments, the company's overall liquidity position is much stronger than the current ratio alone suggests.

The company's fourth quarter trailing 12-month ("TTM") cash from operations was -1.873 billion, and it burned $2.075 billion in TTM FCF. If you divide the CFO's stated 2025 cash and investments of $8.1 billion by its TTM FCF burn rate of $2.075 billion, it equals 3.90 years before the company would run out of cash without raising additional capital. This calculation assumes FCF remains at a steady state over this period. The actual runway can vary depending on changes in capital expenditures ("CapEx"), research and development (R&D) spending, changes in working capital, and seasonal COVID revenue intake.

Investors closely track its cash burn, as it no longer generates enough revenue to cover operations; the rate at which it burns cash on its balance sheet now determines its survival. Investors may punish the stock if FCF declines further or its balance sheet deteriorates rapidly.

Valuation

Although I am about to go through several valuation exercises, be careful using them to make firm buy or sell decisions, as Moderna, like most biotechs, is speculative. The stock will go up and down based more on whether drugs in its pipeline get approved and how successful those drugs become once commercialized, rather than on whether it is "overvalued" or "undervalued."

Moderna has a trailing 12-month ("TTM") price-to-sales (P/S) of 10.17, well above its three-, five-, and seven-year medians. As a result, some investors may consider it overvalued.

The following compares Moderna's two-year forward P/S ratio, two-year forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth (P/S/G), TTM FCF margin, TTM gross margin, TTM operating margin, and TTM return on invested capital (ROIC) with its biotech peers.

Company Name Two-year forward P/S ratio Two-year forward estimated revenue growth Two-year forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth (P/S/G) TTM FCF margin TTM Gross margin TTM Operating Margin TTM Return on Invested Capital ("ROIC")
Moderna 7.55 22.09% 0.34 -107 44.29% -158.1% -26.82%
BioNTech (BNTX) 7.47 7.31% 1.02 8.73% 77.82% -21.78% -5.85%
Arcturus Therapeutics Holdings (ARCT) 4.43 10.08% 0.44 -117.55 95.41% -111.5% -25.43%
GSK (GSK) 2.33 4.75% 0.49 13.25% 72.53% 22.57% 19.46%
Sanofi (SNY) 1.98 6.19% 0.32 16.83% 65.90% 26.10% 6.46%
Merck (MRK) 4.15 5.48% 0.76 19.04% 74.21% 41.11% 20.34%
CRISPR Therapeutics (CRSP) 28.36 212.14% 0.13 -9.9K% -6.53K% -16.19K% -28.00%

Some may consider Moderna overvalued, given its two-year forward P/S ratio trading above the peer median for similar biotech and drug companies. Others may consider Moderna undervalued, given that its P/S/G trades below the peer median.

Moderna's lackluster profitability, FCF, and ROIC relative to peers may justify its lower P/S/G and invalidate its higher P/S ratio. As a result, some may consider the stock fairly valued to overvalued.

Enterprise Value ("EV") to forward sales of 6.42 is higher than the healthcare sector median of 3.12, a sign of potential overvaluation.

The following table shows Moderna's forward price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio and analysts' estimated earnings-per-share ("EPS").

Earlier in this article, I stated, " Moderna may be a hot stock again by 2029." If several of Moderna/Merck's Intismeran autogene mRNA-4157 indications prove commercially successful, Moderna's 2030 EPS growth of 397.92% moves well within the range of possibility, and investors may begin pricing in that type of growth as soon as 2027, if the treatment gets approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and is commercially successful. If you choose to invest in Moderna, be aware that it is a speculative bet on its drug pipeline, and that no valuation method can predict the future. Most of Moderna's future results are based on an unknowable future.

The company has a 2030 forward P/E to analysts' estimated EPS growth ("PEG") ratio of 0.04. If the market prices Moderna at a PEG ratio of 1.0, which is generally considered fair value for a stock, its price would be $1,317.12, a 2,591% rise over its April 2 intraday price of $48.95. However, at this point, take this PEG ratio calculation with a huge grain of salt. The market would never price in that upside six years in advance because so many things could go wrong. I only went through this PEG ratio valuation exercise to illustrate that the upside could be immense over the next several years if everything goes right.

Risks

Moderna's biggest risks are obvious. If the company's phase III trials of mRNA-4157 show poor results, cause a Serious Adverse Event (hurts or kills someone), or commercially underperform, the stock would likely sink like a rock. If the company encounters any hold-ups in bringing material pipeline drugs to market, investors would naturally become very concerned about Moderna's cash burn rate and whether it would need to dilute existing investors through equity raises or load up its balance sheet with more debt.

Delays in getting drugs to market are not simply theoretical. In early February, the U.S. FDA issued an RTF (refusal to file) notice to Moderna for its mRNA-1010 flu vaccine. Moderna's press release stated:

CBER's [Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research] RTF letter, signed by Center Director Vinayak Prasad, MD, MPH, identified the choice of a licensed standard-dose seasonal influenza vaccine comparator as the sole reason for the refusal to initiate the review of Moderna's application. Specifically, the letter cited the lack of an "adequate and well-controlled" study with a comparator arm that "does not reflect the best-available standard of care."

In other words, the FDA believes that comparing the mRNA-1010 flu vaccine to a standard-dose flu vaccine for seniors (65+) was invalid. The FDA claims that a "standard-dose flu vaccine" does not reflect the "best-available standard of care" for seniors. The CDC often prefers high-dose or adjuvanted vaccines for the 65+ age group. Moderna's stance was that the FDA had previously agreed in writing that a standard-dose flu vaccine was "acceptable."

The good news for Moderna was that shortly after receiving the RTF letter, it reached a compromise with the FDA. Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") James Mock said at the TD Cowen 46th Annual Health Care Conference on March 3:

So when we reflected on that [RTF letter], we said we've got to dig in and understand a little bit more, and we requested a Type A meeting. And through that Type A meeting, worked through it with and we came to a good spot. So what is that spot? That spot allows us to be fully approved from ages 50 to 64. And then 65-plus requires a -- will be accelerated approval, should this all be approved. And then we will -- what is required is a post-marketing commitment, which I'll talk about, which was one of your questions. But I mean, the PDUFA date we got is August 5 or 2, which is now 5 months away, which is terrific. That is probably faster than we originally anticipated...

A Type A meeting is a formal meeting between the FDA and a drug company to resolve a dispute over drug approval. A Prescription Drug User Fee Act (PDUFA) date is the deadline by which the FDA must make a decision on the approval of a new drug or biologic application. The government can either approve the drug or reject it, and provide the company with reasons for the rejection and guidance on how to correct any deficiencies.

If all goes well, Moderna will have full approval for adults aged 50 to 64 and accelerated approval for those aged 65 and older by August. Accelerated approval allows mRNA-1010 to reach the market faster, based on early data, while more research continues. The FDA is also requiring Moderna to complete a follow-up study to demonstrate the vaccine's real-world effectiveness as a condition of accelerated approval.

Delays in approval of the flu vaccine can adversely affect the timing of Moderna's combo vaccines, such as the COVID-flu vaccine, potentially throwing off product release timing in its pipeline, and may lead to financial consequences.

Investors should monitor the news for announcements concerning the flu vaccines in the U.S., as a PDUFA date doesn't guarantee approval. If the FDA fully rejects or only partially approves the flu vaccine, the market may react negatively to the announcement.

Another thing that can hold the stock back is any event that increases uncertainty about whether the company can reach breakeven in 2028. If Moderna looks like it can't reach break-even in 2028, the market may begin to worry about increased cash burn, delayed profitability, and whether the company will have to raise additional capital.

Moderna is a Buy

Although Moderna proved mRNA technology during the pandemic, the company and its technology are still speculative. Events could easily go against the company, and the stock could easily get cut in half overnight. People unable to endure substantial losses should avoid the stock.

Still, if you are an investor seeking high returns, Moderna could be in your wheelhouse. The company plans to launch new products in cancer, rare diseases, and infectious diseases to drive revenue growth. If Moderna succeeds with even some of its pipeline, the stock could see double-digit revenue growth by 2027. Suppose the company is also successful with its margin improvement plans; Moderna may see triple-digit EPS growth by 2030. Although long-term estimates are always uncertain, and it's hard to pin down the company’s future value with certainty, patient investors might see considerable profits.

I rate Moderna as a buy for aggressive growth investors willing to speculate on Moderna’s pipeline.

data.title: Protean Funds Scandinavia AB March 2026 Partner Letter
data.description:
Dear Partners,

Protean Small Cap declined by -0.9% in March. The benchmark index declined -3.1%. Since launching in June 2023, the fund has gained 55.6%. The Carnegie Nordic Small Cap Index is up 22.1% in the same period.

The hedge fund Protean Select returned 0.5% in March.

Protean Aktiesparfond Norden returned -2.8%. The benchmark was down by -2.6%. Since inception, twelve months ago, the fund is up 18.3%, and in the same period the VINX Nordic Cap index is up 12.8%. The fund now manages 1.7bn SEK.

All figures are net of fees.

This month's letter elaborates on Aktiesparfonden's encouraging first year, the Fog of War that plagued markets in March, why we remain cautious but not as cautious as mid-March and why we were unusually active. Plus, as always, commentary on the month's various winners and losers.

Thank you for being an investor!

// Team Protean

Nowhere to hide

March 2026 • Written by Pontus Dackmo

Wouldn't it have been convenient to write this month's missive about something completely different? A company deep-dive, a reflection on Nordic small cap valuations, perhaps an observation about the underappreciated capital cycle in some obscure industrial niche. We'd have preferred that. You'd probably have preferred that too.

The instinct, as a Nordic-focused fund manager, is to treat geopolitical events as background noise. We don't trade oil futures, we don't run a macro fund, and we certainly don't have an edge on the intentions of the Iranian Revolutionary Guard or US Commander in Chief. It would be comfortable to dismiss the Middle East crisis as someone else's problem and retreat to the familiar terrain of Nordic company fundamentals.

The problem is: there is no such retreat. An inconvenient fact about the Nordic equity markets is that most of our listed companies have international operations and are deeply embedded in global supply chains. A Swedish industrial company might report in kronor, hold its AGM in Stockholm, and have a thoroughly Nordic board – but its input costs are denominated in dollars, its customers are in Germany and China, and its order book is a function of global capex cycles. An energy shock in the Strait of Hormuz doesn't stop at the Persian Gulf. It travels through Brent crude, through European gas prices, through the front end of rate curves, through the krona, through sentiment, and eventually lands on the desk of a CFO in a mid-sized Swedish town, wondering whether to revise guidance.

There is nowhere to hide from a physical supply shock. Not in Scandinavia, not in small caps, not in "quality compounders".

The fog

General Carl von Clausewitz coined the term "fog of war" almost two centuries ago. In the chaos of conflict, the information available to decision-makers is incomplete, contradictory, and often deliberately

misleading. The rational response, he argued, is not to seek certainty, but to make decisions robust enough to survive being wrong.

March offered a masterclass in modern fog of war, except now the fog is generated not by cannon smoke but by tweets and nonsense.

We have no edge in forecasting the outcome of the Middle East conflict. None. We do not know whether the Strait of Hormuz will reopen next week or remain contested for months. We do not know if escalation leads to a ground operation or if a face-saving deal materializes over Easter. And critically, we don't think anyone else knows either – regardless of how confidently they narrate it. What we can control is how we position for the range of outcomes.

Active Portfolio Management

March was, in all likelihood, the most active month we have had in a long while. Possibly ever.

That warrants some explanation. We do not celebrate turnover. Trading is, at its core, a necessary evil since every ticket is connected to a commission cost borne by the fund, every spread has two sides. We are fully aware that activity for activity's sake is a reliable way to erode returns whilst just producing a feeling of being busy.

What happened in March is that prices moved, on a daily basis, in ways that had very little to do with company fundamentals. Tariff announcements, reversals, threats, walk-backs – the signal-to-noise ratio collapsed. When the market reprices a business by 10% because a politician said something on a Sunday, and then reprices it back 8% two days later, the question we ask ourselves is not "should we trade?" but "can we afford not to?" The disconnect between price and value was, on certain days, wide enough to drive a truck through.

So we traded. We trimmed things that moved too far. We added to things that got dragged down indiscriminately.

We do not fetishize this. The goal is never to be active. It is to own businesses at prices that make sense. Some months that means sitting on our hands. March was not that month. When the market hands you volatility as a gift, and the underlying thesis hasn't changed, the correct response is to use it.

The honest addendum: a fair few trades in March were wrong. Some we were too early on; some we shouldn't have touched at all. Elevated activity is a double-edged sword: more opportunities to profit, but also more chances being wrong.

What we're watching

The honest answer is: we're watching the same thing as everyone else. The Middle East. The Strait of Hormuz. The oil price. But we're also watching for the second-order effects that tend to arrive with a lag and matter more than the headlines.

More speculatively: we are watching for the moment when the market shifts from treating this as a binary event (resolved / not resolved) to treating it as a new baseline. That shift, when it happens, tends to be where opportunity is greatest. Because once the question changes from "will things go back to normal?" to "what does the new normal look like?", the answer requires fundamental analysis rather than geopolitical punditry.

But you don't always need to wait for the fog to lift. Some conclusions are robust regardless of how the binary question resolves. Here's an example from our own process.

In the middle of March, we bought a meaningful amount of Nibe (NDYLF). The thesis is not that the war ends tomorrow, nor that it drags on for months. The thesis is that regardless of outcome, this crisis will put energy efficiency and reduced oil dependence back on the political agenda across Europe – forcefully, and probably durably. It happened after the 2022 Ukraine shock, when heat pump sales exploded and energy renovation became a political priority. That impulse then faded as gas prices normalised and populist backlash pushed governments to soften their climate ambitions. Well, here we are again. Except this time, the lesson should be harder to forget: Europe's energy dependence is not a theoretical risk discussed at think tanks. It's a physical vulnerability that disrupts economies when things go wrong in places we can't control.

Nibe is the dominant European manufacturer of heat pumps. The stock has been in the penalty box for two years – inventory destocking, normalising demand, increasing competition, a weak Swedish housing market. It's down 70% from its peak. We think the market is pricing a permanently impaired business, while the structural case for energy-efficient heating has just been handed another powerful catalyst.

This is the kind of analysis we prefer: not "will there be a ceasefire by April 6th?" but "what is likely true about the world on the other side of this, no matter what?" The best investments tend to come from conclusions that don't depend on the impossible task of predicting the unpredictable.

Aktiesparfond Norden – One Year In

March 31st marked one year since launch. The fund has returned +18.3% against the VBCSKN index's +12.8%. That is an outperformance of 5.5%.

Twelve months is not a track record. It's a start. A better start than a bad start, which is all we'll claim.

The more instructive number is the 1.7bn SEK in assets attracted. To understand why that matters, you need to remember the problem we set out to solve.

The active fund management industry has a well-documented flaw: virtually all serious research shows that actively managed funds underperform their benchmarks after fees. Not because the underlying stock-picking is necessarily bad, but because fees are too high, portfolios too bloated, and the incentive structure backwards. Fund companies optimize for asset gathering. Banks optimize for captive distribution. Neither optimizes for the person actually trying to compound their savings.

The obvious counter is the index fund. Cheap, transparent, honest. Except it owns everything indiscriminately - the frauds, the fads, the structurally declining businesses - with no judgment or overlay.

Our argument has always been that there is a gap between these two options. A white space that nobody has an incentive to fill: a low-fee, genuinely active, long-term oriented fund owning decent Nordic businesses. Just a manager with skin in the game, focused on long-term cash flow generation, making decisions without anyone else's approval required.

The Aktiesparfond was built on the premise that a long-term Nordic saver deserves access to that kind of independent, genuinely active management. At 0.5% per year, with daily liquidity, alongside every expensive active fund and index fund in existence.

That 1.7bn SEK after one year suggests the gap was real. Whether the performance holds is a separate question, the last one year tells us almost nothing with statistical significance. But the structure is working, the thesis is intact, and the alignment of interests remains the foundation.

A Punch In The Mouth

The Prussian military strategist Helmuth von Moltke was a contemporary of Clausewitz. He is remembered for the observation that no plan survives first contact with the enemy (a version of which Mike Tyson famously converted to "Everyone has a plan until they get punched in the mouth."). The investment equivalent is that no portfolio survives first contact with a real-world shock.

We came into 2026 positioned for a world of monetary tailwinds, deregulation, and an improving European outlook. That world has been interrupted – perhaps temporarily, perhaps not.

Months like March are uncomfortable. They are supposed to be. Comfort is rarely where returns are found. We remind ourselves – and you – that our process is designed to compound over years, not months. The key is to stay in the game, avoid big draw-downs, suffer intelligently when suffering is unavoidable, and be ready to act when the fog lifts.

Thank you for being an investor.

// Team Protean

Protean Select

March 2026 • Written by Pontus Dackmo

Protean Select returned +0.5% in March. After two softer months to start the year, this is a welcome result, and more importantly, it is the kind of month that gives the strategy its reason for existing.

Nordic indices were down between 5 and 10% this month, depending on which one you pick. We ran an average net exposure of around 20%. The fund made a small positive return. That is roughly what we designed this thing to do.

We are not a market-neutral fund. Or a short-biased fund. We are a fund that believes, with some conviction, that things generally want to work out in the end, and that the majority of long-term returns are made by owning businesses, not by being overly clever about when to be short them. That belief is why we almost always keep a positive net exposure, even in difficult conditions. The shorts are there not just to make money in isolation, but to act as an airbag: you don't drive with an airbag hoping it deploys, but you're grateful it's there when it does. Like this month.

Biggest contributors were the OMX future short position (plus put-spread), Rusta long, NIBE long and Electrolux (ELUXY) short, in that order.

Biggest detractors were Devyser , Lundin Mining (LUNMF) (although offset by a short in Boliden (BDNNY)), Volvo (VLVLY) and Getinge (GNGBY) long positions.

We enter April at 19% net exposure. Gross is now at 117%, having nudged it upward from more cautious levels, moving gradually back toward our historical average of around 135%. This is a deliberate, incremental decision, not a conviction call. The market feels non-linear right now. If conditions continue to feel constructive enough to justify additional risk, we will add. If not, we won't.

We said going into the year that we wanted to earn the right to take more risk. March was a small step in that direction. Let's see what April has to say about it.

Lowering the capacity limit

We announced during the month that we are lowering the capacity limit for Protean Select from SEK 2 billion to SEK 1 billion. The fund currently manages approximately SEK 970 million. We are, in other words, very close.

Some context. When we originally set the cap at SEK 2 billion, we assessed capacity based on the fund in isolation. That was reasonable at the time. But circumstances have changed. We now manage institutional mandates alongside the fund, applying a similar investment strategy in the same universe of Nordic small- and midcap companies. Running larger aggregate capital through the same opportunity set degrades the things that matter most: execution quality, the ability to take meaningful positions in less liquid names, and the speed of decision-making that comes with being small. We'd rather close too early than too late.

Here is how it will work. Once we pass SEK 1 billion, we will communicate that the fund is closing. There will then be a final subscription window of approximately one month. After that, the fund is closed to new capital. Withdrawals are always possible – this only affects new deposits.

Readers of these letters will recognise the philosophy.

We have said since day one that we optimise for performance, not for convenience, size, or marketing. We have written at length - perhaps at tedious length - about the perverse incentives in fund management, how AUM-maximising behaviour dilutes returns, and how size is one of the primary ways performance dies. We have said we would cap the fund early. We have said we would sacrifice revenue for return quality. And now, approaching the moment where those words become action, it feels worth pausing to acknowledge what this means.

On a personal level, the prospect of actually closing the fund is a source of pride. Not because turning away capital is clever business – it obviously isn't. But because it means we are doing what we said we would do. When we started Protean, the motivation was to invest our own savings in an institutional setting, not to build an asset gathering machine. Every structural choice we've made has pointed in this direction: the quarterly redemptions that scare off allocators, the size cap that limits our fee income, the high hurdle rates that make performance fees genuinely hard to earn. None of this is "How to Build a Big Profitable Fund Management Business". If there were a textbook on the subject, we'd feature as a cautionary tale.

But here's the thing. Almost four years in, the fund has delivered competitive risk-adjusted returns. We even won an actual prize for it. Assets have grown to the cap not through marketing campaigns, but through performance and word of mouth. Our investors include some of the most sophisticated allocators we know, alongside friends, family, and our own savings. That this motley crew has collectively brought us to the point of closure feels like vindication of a philosophy.

We recognise this limits flexibility for both existing and prospective investors. That trade-off is deliberate. Protecting the conditions for good long-term returns is, in our view, the most important thing we can do for the people who have already entrusted us with their capital.

Protean Small Cap

March 2026 • Written by Carl Gustafsson

Protean Small Cap returned -0.9% in March. Our benchmark CSRXN (SEK) was down -3.1% during the month. Hence, the fund outperformed the index by 2.3%. Since inception in June 2023, the fund has outperformed the index by 33.5%. Total performance since inception is 55.6% net of fees.

The fund now manages c. SEK 990m following a continued inflow of funds, thank you for believing in us.

March recap

Top contributors were Cint, Rusta, BTS, Smartoptics and Nibe. Notable detractors were Devyser, Balder (BALDY), Arctic Falls, Nyfosa and ITAB.

Cint reported a considerable sequential improvement in sales during the fourth quarter and we increased our stake in the company on the day of the report in February. Since then, the share has continued to climb and it became our biggest contributor in March. It's not a stock for widows and orphans but we believe the market is underestimating the growth opportunity within the business area Media Measurement. We acknowledge a string of operational issues, as well as about the impact of AI on the market research sector, but Q3 was likely the trough in terms of pain. The valuation is very appealing.

Rusta reported a very strong quarter, where the benefits of the stronger SEK started to have an impact. Healthy LFL, as well as a firmer outlook for store openings, lead to share to gain in an otherwise gloomy March for consumer exposure.

On the detractor side, Devyser continued its slump, with the share down by a third year to date. As we write this, the share now trades below its IPO price from 2021, despite clearly approaching cash-flow break-even and strong growth prospects in several areas. Devyser is active within genetic testing where DNA is used to assess disease risk as well as detecting them. Introducing new testing protocols takes time, but they are very sticky once established. The market might have overestimated the speed

data.title: Hercules Capital: Cheap With Massive Caveats
data.description:
Shares of business development company Hercules Capital, Inc. (HTGC) have fallen roughly 30% since February 2025, primarily on accelerating worries around the private credit and BDC sectors. Investors are also increasingly concerned about these lenders large exposure to the software sector due to that industry's perceived vulnerability to AI disruption and low recovery rates in the event of defaults.

That said, the 35% of Hercules' debt portfolio in technology focuses on software companies with hardware moats or highly regulated customer bases with no pure-play AI or data center exposure. With a 12.6% yield buttressed by $0.82 per share of spillover income and trading at 1.25 times an NAV that rose 4% in 2025, the recent broad-based insider buying in Hercules merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Hercules Capital, Inc. is a San Mateo, California-based specialty finance concern primarily focused on providing floating-rate structured or senior secured capital to the life sciences and technology sectors. Since its inception, the firm has originated over 700 loans and equity investments totaling more than $25 billion. It also has a small but growing advisory business for private credit. Hercules was formed in 2003 and went public in 2005, raising net proceeds of $70.9 million at $13 per share. Its stock trades just under $15.00 a share, translating to an approximate market cap of $2.7 billion.

Business Development Companies

Structured as an internally managed business development company, or BDC, the firm is the seventh largest by market cap and one of 13 with a value north of $1 billion. BDCs are closed-end investment companies that provide capital to middle-market businesses, stepping in to provide financing when more conventional methods, such as bank loans or bond issuances, are unavailable. BDCs could be characterized as either venture capital/private equity debt funds for the public, banks with a different capital structure, or a junk bond portfolio REIT. Substantially all BDCs elect to be treated as regulated investment companies (RICs), which means if it distributes at least 90% of its taxable income to shareholders, it pays no income tax—similar to a mortgage REIT. Because of the pass-through nature of the RIC, BDCs cannot use earnings to grow their capital bases, meaning they must execute additional equity and/or debt issuances to grow organically. BDCs can be either internally managed or externally managed, with both approaches having attendant advantages and disadvantages.

Whereas banks leverage up their equity capital in the form of deposits by a factor of 10:1 and make a net interest spread on the interest charged on their loans above the interest paid to their depositors, BDCs like Hercules leverage debt financing in the form of collateralized loan obligations, unsecured notes, and revolving credit facilities to make a spread on the interest charged to their customers over their own interest obligations. Until 2018, the maximum debt-to-equity ratio for BDCs was mandated at 1:1. With the passage of the 2018 Small Business Credit Availability Act, it changed it to 2:1. Owing to their ability to substantially leverage their balance sheets, banks enjoy a much higher return on equity than their BDC counterparts, which the latter partially offsets through superior efficiency ratios and no income tax burden.

Approach

Hercules primarily provides structured debt to businesses that have been in operation for at least six months with either revenue generation or an anticipation of revenue generation in the subsequent 24 to 48 months. The preponderance is sponsored by venture capital or private equity. They are typically collateralized by a security interest in available assets (usually intellectual property) and range from $25 million to $100 million, with some exceptions. At YE25, 98% of the firm’s loans were variable (84% featuring a prime rate floor), with interest rates generally ranging from 7% to 14%.

Its debt is usually structured with warrants, equity, options, or other rights to convert into common or preferred ownership interest. The maturity is typically between two and five years. At YE25, Hercules held warrant and/or equity securities in 164 businesses valued at $180.2 million. A smaller portion of the firm’s portfolio consists of straight senior secured debt, collateralized by accounts receivable that generally feature a term of three to five years. Nearly 90% of Hercules' debt is first lien senior secured.

The firm’s $4.26 billion debt portfolio covering 127 businesses at YE25 was comprised chiefly of loans to application software (24% of total), drug discovery and development (23%), healthcare services (19%), systems software (with IT clients) (11%), as well as consumer and business services (10%) verticals. Hercules does not invest in oil and gas, metals or minerals, pure AI or data centers, commercial or residential mortgage-backed securities, or crypto. Its weighted average effective yield, which includes prepayments and associated fees, was 13.3% in FY25 (down from 14.4% in FY24), while net interest margin [NIM] averaged 10.8% in FY25, down from 12.5% in FY24.

As stated previously, the source of the firm’s capital comes from equity and debt issuances, with the former providing $2.22 billion and the latter encompassing $2.31 billion, carrying coupon rates of 2.625% to 6.00% as of December 31, 2025.

Hercules self-rates its investments on a one-to-five scale, with grade 1 representing the least risk; 2, performing in line with expectations; 3, risk has materially increased since origination; 4, interest and principal of the loan are at risk; and 5, the borrower is in workout with some or all principal loss anticipated. Using these ratings, the performance of the firm’s portfolio has been consistent with a weighted average investment grade of 2.20 at YE25, representing a gain over 2.27 at September 30, 2025, as well as improvements versus 2.26 at YE24 and 2.24 at YE23. As of December 31, 2025, no loans were rated 5, and only $73.3 million (1.7%) were rated 4.

Share Price Performance

Structured as a BDC, investors care more about the size and safety of the dividend. That said, after achieving an all-time high of $22.04 in February 2025, shares of HTGC have fallen 34% due to several factors. First, at their peak, they were trading at a significant 1.89 times premium to net asset value. Second, with interest rates dropping during 2025, the firm’s effective yield and net interest margin fell to their lowest levels since mid-2022. Third, with its portfolio 34.9% exposed to software concerns (up from 29.5% at YE24), Hercules’ stock succumbed to the AI-induced SaaSpocalypse fears gripping the market in the first quarter.

4Q25 Financials & FY26 Outlook

That software exposure was addressed on the firm’s conference call after reporting 4Q25 financials on February 12, 2026, in which Hercules posted net investment income (NII) of $0.48 per share (GAAP) on total investment income of $137.4 million versus NII of $0.49 per share (GAAP) on total investment income of $121.8 million in 4Q24, representing a 2% decline on a gain of 13%. Its top line was buoyed by a 22% year-over-year increase in investments to $4.46 billion, which was bolstered by a 71% year-over-year increase in originations to $1.06 billion. Its bottom line was held back by lower NIM due to lower rates.

Those results brought FY25 NII to $1.91 per share (GAAP) on total investment income of $532.5 million, representing a decline of 5% on an 8% gain over FY24. New debt and equity commitments totaled $3.92 billion, up 46% year-over-year, while gross funding of $2.28 billion represented 26% growth compared to FY24 -- both firm records.

The bottom line missed consensus by $0.01, while the top line was $4.1 million light. Also of note were early loan repayments of $149.7 million, which as a percentage of the loan portfolio (3.5%) represented the lowest total since 2Q22, acting as a drag on NII. Furthermore, payment-in-kind (PIK) interest income, a potential sign of loan performance deterioration, was up 7% year-over-year to $14.3 million and 10% of the total. That said, 86% of PIK income in 4Q25 was a feature of the original underwriting.

As for concerns over its software exposure, management stated that its underwriting focus was on software concerns with either a hardware moat or with highly regulated customer bases, reiterating its non-exposure to pure-AI or data center originations. It also offered that 69% of its new commitments (although only half its funding) in 4Q25 were to the life sciences businesses, with the balance to technology.

It also articulated that the volatile environment enabled better underwriting spreads, which it was using to sign higher-quality clients on the risk spectrum without hurting returns. That said, it also mentioned that it was “continuing to see deals get done in the market without strong structure and well outside what we believe are prudent underwriting metrics for the asset class.” With nearly three-quarters of its prime-based loans at their floors, a further 75-basis-point decline in the rate would negatively impact its bottom line by $0.04 per share, whereas a 75-basis point increase would help by $0.07.

That said, management guided core yield, which excludes the benefit of income (prepayment fees) recognized from early loan repayments, other one-time fees, and bank interest income, would decline from the 12.5% level of 4Q25 due to the full impact of the most recent Fed rate cut on its floating-rate loans.

Despite Hercules’ conservative tone, the revelation that software loans now comprised 35% of its portfolio (up from 29.5% in the prior year) and no increase in its supplemental dividend—discussed below—prompted a Piper Sandler downgrade and lower estimates from other sell-side analysts, triggering a 6% selloff in shares of HTGC to $15.58 in the subsequent trading session. The stock has fallen five percent since.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary

That said, the firm had no issue bolstering its liquidity on February 10, 2026, raising gross proceeds of $300 million in a 5.35% debt offering due 2029, bringing its total available liquidity to ~$825 million (~$357 million cash). Its balance sheet is in solid stead with GAAP leverage on December 31, 2025, at a relatively conservative 104.4%, up from 99.5% on September 30, 2025, but still at the lower end of its historical 100% to 115% range. Despite the sharp decline in share price, Hercules’ NAV rose 4% during 2025 to $12.13 per share.

As for its dividend, Hercules pays a base distribution of $0.40 per share, which has been augmented for 22 consecutive quarters by a supplemental dividend. Despite the headwinds from lower rates, management stated that it would provide four quarterly distributions of $0.47 per share in 2026—the same as 2025—while maintaining $0.82 per share ($149.9 million) of spillover income. Owing to the steep decline in their value over the past 13 months, shares of HTGC currently yield a noteworthy 12.6%.

The Street is mixed on its view on Hercules, as can be seen above. On average, sell-side analysts expect the firm to generate NII of $1.93 per share (down from $2.02 per share before the 4Q25 report) on total investment income of $581 million in FY26, followed by $1.96 per share on total investment income of $617 million in FY27.

There were some significant insider purchases in the stock during March, it should be noted.

Verdict

With SaaSpocalypse fears gripping the market, venture capital fundraising in 2025 was at its lowest level since 2019, which could impact the number of opportunities entering Hercules’ funnel over the next two years. That said, shares of HTGC trade at their skinniest premium to NAV (1.23 times) this decade. With a 12.6% yield, a $0.47 per share quarterly dividend in 2026 all but guaranteed by $0.82 a share of spillover income, Hercules would ordinarily make a solid covered call candidate or a purchase for income-oriented investors.

However, there is very little liquidity in the options against the equity, rendering a covered call strategy non-viable. And given the current troubles in the overall BDC and private credit space, I don't want to take a chance that HTGC and others in the sector become even more like a 'falling knife.' Therefore, I am not taking a position in HTGC, despite its juicy yield.
